chunks.title: The CIA triad
chunks.text: Cybersecurity is anchored on three goals — Confidentiality (only authorized parties can read data), Integrity (data is not tampered with in transit or at rest), and Availability (the system remains usable). Different threats target different goals: eavesdropping attacks confidentiality, message tampering attacks integrity, denial-of-service attacks availability. Designs should consider all three; defending only one leaves obvious gaps.

chunks.title: Symmetric cryptography
chunks.text: Symmetric encryption uses the same secret key for encryption and decryption. AES is the standard block cipher, operating on 128-bit blocks with 128/192/256-bit keys. ChaCha20 is a stream cipher used widely on mobile. Modes of operation (CBC, CTR, GCM) determine how blocks are chained. GCM also provides authentication. The challenge with symmetric crypto is key distribution — how do two parties agree on a key over an insecure channel?

chunks.title: Public-key cryptography
chunks.text: Public-key (asymmetric) crypto uses a key pair: a public key anyone can know, and a private key kept secret. RSA and ECC are common families. Anyone can encrypt with the public key; only the private-key holder can decrypt. The system also supports digital signatures: the private-key holder signs, anyone can verify with the public key. TLS uses public-key crypto to negotiate a symmetric session key, then switches to symmetric crypto for speed.

chunks.title: Hashing and password storage
chunks.text: A cryptographic hash function maps any input to a fixed-size output that is one-way (can't invert), collision-resistant, and avalanches (small input change → big output change). SHA-256 is standard. Passwords should never be stored in plaintext or even plain-hashed — use a slow, memory-hard KDF like bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2 with a per-user salt. The salt prevents rainbow-table attacks; the slowness limits brute-force speed.

chunks.title: Authentication and authorization
chunks.text: Authentication answers 'who are you?' (passwords, OTP, biometric, hardware tokens, certificates). Authorization answers 'what are you allowed to do?' (RBAC — role-based; ABAC — attribute-based). Multi-factor auth combines two of: something you know, have, are. OAuth 2.0 delegates authorization between services without sharing passwords. JWT is a common token format carrying user identity and claims, signed by the issuer.

chunks.title: OWASP Top 10 web vulnerabilities
chunks.text: OWASP's Top 10 catalogues the most common web app risks. SQL injection: unsanitized user input concatenated into queries (fix: parameterized queries). XSS: untrusted input rendered in HTML (fix: escape on output). CSRF: a malicious site triggers actions on a logged-in user's behalf (fix: CSRF tokens, SameSite cookies). Broken authentication, insecure deserialization, security misconfiguration, and using components with known vulnerabilities round out the list.

chunks.title: Network attacks and defences
chunks.text: Common network attacks: DDoS overwhelms a service with traffic (defence: rate limiting, CDNs, scrubbing services). Man-in-the-middle intercepts traffic (defence: TLS, certificate pinning). DNS spoofing redirects users (defence: DNSSEC). ARP poisoning on local networks (defence: dynamic ARP inspection). Port scanning probes for open services (defence: firewall, intrusion detection). Defence in depth means layering multiple controls so no single failure compromises everything.

chunks.title: Threat modelling
chunks.text: Threat modelling identifies what could go wrong before code is written. STRIDE categorizes threats: Spoofing identity, Tampering with data, Repudiation, Information disclosure, Denial of service, Elevation of privilege. Walk through the data flow diagram of your system and ask which STRIDE threats apply to each component. The output is a prioritized list of mitigations. Doing this once during design beats firefighting in production.

chunks.title: Penetration testing
chunks.text: Pen testing simulates an attacker against your systems to find vulnerabilities before real adversaries do. Phases: reconnaissance (gather info), scanning (find services and known vulns), exploitation (try to break in), post-exploitation (what can the attacker do once in), reporting. Black-box pen testers know nothing about the target; white-box have source and architecture. Annual pen tests are a compliance baseline for PCI-DSS, ISO 27001.

chunks.title: Vulnerability scanning
chunks.text: Vulnerability scanners (Nessus, OpenVAS, Qualys) probe systems for known CVEs by sending crafted requests and matching response signatures. They cover OS patches, network services, web apps. Scans produce false positives — confirmation often requires manual verification. SAST scans source code; DAST scans running applications; IAST instruments the running application for higher fidelity.

chunks.title: CVE and CVSS
chunks.text: CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures) gives every disclosed vulnerability a unique ID (CVE-2024-12345). CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System) rates severity from 0-10 based on attack vector, complexity, privileges required, impact on CIA. Critical (9-10) demands immediate patching; low (0-3) often can wait. Newer EPSS predicts likelihood of exploitation, helping prioritize the firehose of vulns.

chunks.title: Patch management
chunks.text: Patching closes known vulnerabilities. Strategy: inventory all software, subscribe to vendor advisories, test critical patches in staging, deploy in priority order. Automated patching for OS and library updates; manual for application changes. The window between disclosure and exploit is shrinking (sometimes hours), so latency matters. Unpatched systems are the leading cause of breaches.

chunks.title: Zero-day exploits
chunks.text: A zero-day is a vulnerability the vendor doesn't know about yet — defenders have had zero days to patch. Highly valuable on black markets and to state actors. Defenses against unknown vulnerabilities: defense in depth, least privilege, sandboxing, exploit mitigation (ASLR, DEP, stack canaries), behaviour-based detection. Bug bounty programs incentivize responsible disclosure.

chunks.title: Buffer overflows
chunks.text: A buffer overflow writes past the end of an allocated buffer, corrupting adjacent memory — often the return address on the stack. An attacker overwrites it with the address of malicious code (shellcode). Modern mitigations: stack canaries (random value before the return address; corruption detected), DEP (data pages aren't executable), ASLR (randomize memory layout). Memory-safe languages (Rust, Go) eliminate the class entirely.

chunks.title: Return-oriented programming (ROP)
chunks.text: When DEP prevents executing shellcode, attackers reuse existing executable code: ROP chains together small instruction sequences ending in 'ret' (gadgets) to perform arbitrary computation. Each gadget pulls its next address from the controlled stack. Defenses: ASLR (gadgets move), CFI (control-flow integrity) which verifies indirect jumps land at legal targets.

chunks.title: Format string vulnerabilities
chunks.text: C's printf-family functions interpret format specifiers (%s, %x, %n) in the format string. If user input is passed as the format string, attackers can read arbitrary memory (%x) or write arbitrary memory (%n). Always use printf("%s", user_input) — never printf(user_input). Static analyzers flag this pattern; modern compilers warn at -Wformat-security.

chunks.title: Code injection
chunks.text: Code injection is when user input becomes executable code. SQL injection puts SQL in queries; command injection puts shell commands in system() calls; LDAP injection in directory queries; XPath injection in XML queries. Fix: parameterized queries / prepared statements wherever the platform supports them. Never build executable strings from concatenation.

chunks.title: Privilege escalation
chunks.text: An attacker who gets in usually starts with limited access and tries to escalate to root or admin. Vertical escalation gets higher privilege on the same machine (kernel exploit, sudo misconfiguration, SUID binary abuse). Horizontal escalation jumps to other users at the same level (steal credentials, exploit shared resources). Mitigations: least privilege, strict sudo policy, regular audit of SUID files.

chunks.title: Rootkits
chunks.text: A rootkit is malware that hides itself and grants persistent root access. User-mode rootkits replace system binaries (ls, ps) to hide files and processes; kernel-mode rootkits patch the kernel itself, undetectable by user-space tools. Firmware rootkits live below the OS entirely. Detection requires booting from clean media; cure usually means full reinstall. Secure boot and signed kernels make rootkits harder to install.

chunks.title: Ransomware
chunks.text: Ransomware encrypts a victim's files and demands payment for the decryption key. Modern ransomware also exfiltrates data and threatens public release (double extortion). Spreads via phishing, RDP brute force, unpatched VPNs. Defense: tested backups (3-2-1 rule — three copies, two media, one offsite), endpoint detection, MFA on remote access, segmented networks. Paying ransom doesn't guarantee recovery and funds future attacks.

chunks.title: Phishing and social engineering
chunks.text: Phishing tricks users into revealing credentials or running malware via email, SMS (smishing), or voice (vishing). Spear phishing targets specific individuals with tailored lures. Defense: user training, email gateways with link rewriting, MFA (steals one password, can't log in), DMARC/SPF/DKIM (reject spoofed email). Annual training reduces click rates from ~30% to under 10%.

chunks.title: IDS and IPS
chunks.text: Intrusion Detection Systems monitor network or host traffic and alert on suspicious patterns. Intrusion Prevention Systems take action — drop the packet, terminate the connection. Signature-based (Snort, Suricata) matches known attack patterns. Anomaly-based learns normal behaviour and flags deviations. Modern XDR (Extended Detection and Response) correlates signals across endpoints, network, identity.

chunks.title: SIEM
chunks.text: A SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) aggregates logs from across the environment — firewalls, endpoints, applications, identity systems — and correlates them to detect attacks. Examples: Splunk, ELK with security extensions, Microsoft Sentinel, Chronicle. The SOC (Security Operations Center) uses the SIEM as their console. Good SIEM tuning is critical — too many alerts and analysts ignore them.

chunks.title: Compliance frameworks
chunks.text: ISO 27001 is the international standard for information security management systems. SOC 2 covers service organisations on five trust principles. PCI-DSS applies to anyone handling payment cards. HIPAA covers health data in the US. GDPR governs personal data of EU residents. Each demands documented controls, periodic audits, breach disclosure. Compliance ≠ security, but it forces a baseline of practices.

chunks.title: GDPR and privacy laws
chunks.text: GDPR (EU) requires lawful basis for processing personal data, data subject rights (access, deletion, portability), 72-hour breach notification, and privacy by design. Fines reach 4% of global revenue. Similar laws: CCPA (California), LGPD (Brazil), DPDP (India). Practical effects: data inventory, retention policies, DPO appointment, vendor data processing agreements, cookie consent banners.

chunks.title: Zero trust architecture
chunks.text: Zero trust replaces 'trust the internal network' with 'verify every request'. Every service-to-service call authenticates and authorizes; network location alone gives no privilege. Implemented via service mesh, identity-aware proxies, short-lived credentials, mTLS. Google's BeyondCorp is the canonical implementation. Migration is incremental — most enterprises run hybrid for years.

chunks.title: Endpoint security
chunks.text: Endpoints (laptops, phones, servers) are where most attacks land. Modern EDR (Endpoint Detection and Response) — CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Defender — combines antivirus with behaviour monitoring, threat hunting, isolation. Critical controls: full-disk encryption, screen lock, patched OS, MFA, no local admin for daily use. Mobile device management enforces policy across fleets.

chunks.title: Container and cloud security
chunks.text: Container security covers image vulnerabilities (scan base images), runtime threats (block unexpected syscalls with seccomp/AppArmor), and secrets management (don't bake into images). Cloud security adds shared-responsibility issues: the cloud provider secures the infrastructure; you secure your configuration, IAM, data. Misconfigured S3 buckets and over-permissive IAM roles are the most common cloud breach causes.

chunks.title: Incident response
chunks.text: When a security incident hits, follow NIST's PICERL: Preparation, Identification, Containment, Eradication, Recovery, Lessons Learned. Containment options: isolate the host, rotate credentials, block IPs. Eradication removes the root cause. Recovery restores from clean backups. Lessons learned drives improvements. Tabletop exercises rehearse scenarios so the team isn't winging it under pressure.

chunks.title: Digital forensics
chunks.text: Forensics preserves and analyses digital evidence. Chain of custody documents who handled the evidence and when. Memory forensics (Volatility) examines RAM for processes, network connections, injected code. Disk forensics (Autopsy, EnCase) finds deleted files and timeline of activity. Network forensics analyses pcap captures. Done correctly, forensic evidence stands up in court.

chunks.title: Cryptographic protocols
chunks.text: Protocols layer crypto primitives into useful applications. TLS for transport. PGP/GPG for messages and files. SSH for remote terminals. Signal Protocol for end-to-end encrypted messaging (used by WhatsApp, Signal). Each protocol negotiates algorithms, exchanges keys, authenticates parties, encrypts data. Designing protocols is hard — most weaknesses come from protocol flaws, not algorithm flaws.

chunks.title: Key management
chunks.text: Key management is the hardest part of practical cryptography. Generate keys with strong randomness. Store them in HSMs (Hardware Security Modules) or managed services (AWS KMS, GCP Cloud KMS) that never expose the raw key. Rotate keys periodically. Have revocation procedures for compromise. Separate keys by purpose and environment. Most breaches involving crypto are key-management failures, not algorithm breaks.

chunks.title: Security headers
chunks.text: HTTP security headers harden web apps. Content-Security-Policy restricts which sources can load scripts and styles (mitigates XSS). Strict-Transport-Security forces HTTPS. X-Frame-Options prevents clickjacking. X-Content-Type-Options blocks MIME sniffing. Referrer-Policy limits referrer leakage. Permissions-Policy controls browser feature access. securityheaders.com grades any URL on these in seconds.

chunks.title: Bug bounty programs
chunks.text: Bug bounty programs pay researchers for responsibly disclosing vulnerabilities. Run by individual companies (Google VRP, Apple) or via platforms (HackerOne, Bugcrowd). Scope and rules define what's in/out of bounds. Severity buckets define payment. Benefits: more eyes than any internal team can match, deterrent against black-market sales. Programs need triage capacity — accepting a report and ignoring it is worse than not running one.
